title: CSS Specificity
content:
### CSS Specificity **Specificity** determines which CSS rule wins when multiple rules target the same element. --- ### Specificity Hierarchy | Level | Selector Type | Example | |-------|--------------|---------| | 1 (lowest) | Element selectors | `p`, `h1`, `div` | | 2 | Class, attribute, pseudo-class | `.class`, `[type]`, `:hover` | | 3 | ID selectors | `#header` | | 4 | Inline styles | `style="..."` | | 5 (highest) | `!important` | `color: red !important;` | --- ### Specificity Calculation Count each selector type as a score `(inline, ID, class, element)`: ```css p /* (0, 0, 0, 1) */ .text /* (0, 0, 1, 0) */ #header /* (0, 1, 0, 0) */ p.text /* (0, 0, 1, 1) */ #nav a.active /* (0, 1, 1, 1) */ ``` Higher score wins. --- ### Example ```html

Text

``` ```css p { color: gray; } /* Loses to class */ .highlight { color: blue; } /* Loses to ID */ #intro { color: orange; } /* Loses to inline */ /* inline style: green */ /* Loses to !important */ #intro { color: red !important; } /* Wins! */ ``` --- ### The `!important` Rule ```css p { color: red !important; } ``` Overrides all other rules. Use **sparingly**. > **Best Practice:** Avoid `!important` when possible. Use specific selectors instead. It makes debugging harder.
